narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 41% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 school decile of 4—below the typical student's 5.71—and your Year 3 decile of 3, even lower than the average 5.53. Your LSAT score of 27 is notably below the dataset average of 36.7, your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 trails the usual 3.21, and your middle family income aligns roughly with the middle of the pack around 3.47. As a white male planning to attend part-time—a less common choice at just 7.3% compared to 92.7% full-time students—these details painted a picture of higher risk in the model's eyes. When explaining this decision, the strongest influence came from your Year 3 decile, which nudged the prediction toward failure more than any other factor. Right behind it were your Year 1 decile and LSAT score, both contributing significantly to the elevated risk assessment. Your family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, part-time status, and gender followed in that order, each adding a smaller but still positive push toward the failure prediction—meaning none strongly favored passing in your case. Compared to typical students who pass, your grades and test scores are in the lower deciles and well below averages, setting you apart from the majority who succeed on their first bar try. While demographics like gender and race didn't sway things much here, your part-time plans stood out as atypical and slightly riskier. The good news is you can take actionable steps: consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above 36.7, as it's a key factor you can directly improve. Switching to full-time study might also help align you with the stronger profiles in our data. Building a higher undergraduate GPA through post-bac coursework or similar could address that gap too, though past deciles are fixed. With targeted improvements like these, you could shift future predictions in your favor and strengthen your path to passing the bar. Keep pushing—you've got the determination to turn this around.
